summary: DeVault explains how reflection works in Hare (redacted as 'xxxx'), his new systems programming language. The post walks through the user-facing API where a 'type' meta-type can be converted to a typeinfo pointer via types::reflect, which is simply a pointer cast. The compiler statically allocates typeinfo structures into the binary, with built-in types provided by the runtime and complex types generated at compile time. Type aliases enable singleton typeinfo allocations that the linker can strip if unused, while anonymous structs must be regenerated each time they appear.
key_points: Reflection in Hare works by statically allocating typeinfo structures at compile time and returning pointers to them, making types::reflect a simple pointer cast
Built-in types have pre-allocated typeinfo in the runtime module, while complex types like structs get their typeinfo generated by the compiler
Type aliases serve double duty as both naming conveniences and optimization points, enabling singleton typeinfo allocations that unused code paths can strip via linker gc-sections
The repr field uses a tagged union to represent all possible type representations in the type system, from builtins to structs to function pointers
Future work includes a 'value' abstraction for arbitrary types and integration with the language's own parser and type checker modules
money_quotes: It simply casts the 'type' value to a pointer, which is what it is.

When the compiler sees an expression like let x = type(int), it statically allocates the typeinfo data structure into the program and returns a pointer to it, which is then wrapped up in the opaque 'type' meta-type.
The declaration of a type alias provides us with the perfect opportunity to statically allocate a typeinfo singleton for it.
Any of these which go unused by the program are automatically stripped out by the linker thanks to the --gc-sections flag.
This has the unfortunate effect of re-generating all of these typeinfo structures every time someone uses type(struct { x: int, y: int }).
key_insight: Hare's reflection system achieves zero runtime overhead by statically allocating type information at compile time and reducing reflection to simple pointer casts, with the linker stripping unused type data.
